title: St. Epiphanius (Departure) — St. Solochon & St. Andronicus (Monthly)
narrative:
The Blessed Repose of St. Epiphanius of Cyprus the Theologian, St. Solochon the Soldier-Martyr, and St. Andronicus the Apostle of the Seventy

On the seventeenth of Ginbot, the holy Church commemorates the blessed repose of Saint Epiphanius, Archbishop of Cyprus and tireless doctor of the faith, whose departure from this life took place on board a ship returning him from Constantinople to his see — a detail the tradition finds theologically significant, for Epiphanius died in transit, between destinations, as he had lived, never truly settled in any fixed position but always moving between controversies, between councils, between the demands of a Church that needed his encyclopaedic knowledge and his uncompromising clarity. He died on the water, as if the sea were the appropriate setting for the repose of one who had never found in this world the solid ground that his searching spirit required. The tradition celebrates his departure as the moment when the restlessness of his enormous zeal was finally stilled, and the man who had catalogued eighty heresies found at last the single uncontested truth he had been defending all along.

The legacy of Epiphanius for the Ethiopian tradition is concentrated in his biblical scholarship: his work on the weights and measures of Scripture, on the Hebrew names of the books, on the interpretation of difficult passages, formed part of the educational tradition of the Alexandrian church and through that tradition reached Ethiopia, where his learning about the physical world of the Bible — the geography, the botany, the zoology of the Holy Land — was received as a kind of sacred natural history, a knowledge of the creation as it appears in the pages of the sacred text. His departure alongside the monthly feast of Andronicus is the tradition's pairing of the encyclopaedist and the apostle: the man who knew everything about the text and the man who embodied it.

Saint Solochon is the great discovery of this day and one of the most poignant of the soldier-martyrs: a member of a military unit stationed in Chalcedon who was one of three soldiers — himself, Pamphamer, and Pamphylos — identified as Christians and brought to the altar of the military gods for the sacrificial oath. All three refused; Solochon was the last to be brought forward, and by the time it was his turn he had watched his two companions beaten to the edge of death. The tradition records that when the soldiers attempted to force him to make the sacrificial gesture by grabbing his hand and moving it toward the altar, Solochon bit off his own fingers at the knuckle so that the hand that had been forced could not be used. This act — violent, irreversible, permanently disfiguring, simultaneously a refusal and a sacrifice — is one of the most extreme acts of bodily self-determination in the martyrological tradition, and the tradition receives it not as fanaticism but as the most literal possible enactment of the Matthean saying: if your hand causes you to sin, cut it off. It is better to enter life maimed than to go to hell with two hands.

Saint Andronicus receives his monthly commemoration on this day: one of the Seventy Apostles named by Paul in the letter to the Romans, where Paul identifies him and his companion Junia as kinsmen who were in Christ before Paul himself, and as notable among the apostles. Andronicus is therefore among the very first Christians named in the Pauline letters, a figure of the earliest Jerusalem community whose witness preceded Paul's own conversion. The tradition of his subsequent missionary activity places him in various regions of the Roman Empire, and his monthly feast is the Church's regular return to the question of origins: who were the first? What did the community look like before Paul gave it its theology? Andronicus and Junia are the tradition's answer — ordinary believers, kinsmen in the body and in the faith, present before the great apostle arrived, witnesses to a Christianity that preceded its own systematisation. Their monthly feast is the Church's memory of its pre-systematic self. Amen.
